clean_story:
Sunlight as brain fuel. Shadows shift emotions.

The term light therapy comes from using artificial light to mimic the sun’s mood-lifting effects—because light isn’t just for seeing. It’s a chemical remote control.

Your eyes don’t just send images to your brain. Special cells detect brightness like a thermostat, signaling your body: "Wake up" (blue-rich morning light) or "Wind down" (reddish sunset). This triggers serotonin—a calm-focus chemical—or melatonin, the sleep switch. Cloudy winters? Less sunlight means less serotonin, leaving some people sluggish or irritable. Hospitals use bright white lights to stabilize patients’ moods; prisons test blue-tinted rooms to reduce aggression.

A 2017 study measured office workers under cool vs. warm light. Cool light (like overcast noon) boosted alertness by 18%, but warm light (like a fire’s glow) cut stress hormones by 12%. Even your phone’s "night mode" isn’t just easier on the eyes—it’s a tiny guard against mood dips.

Three related subjects are circadian rhythm hacks, color psychology tricks, and how blind people sense light.
